metadata.title: Why Blocking the Accelerator Perfectly Can Still Fail
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 02. Mechanism: a targeted drug perfectly blocks its oncogene driver but the tumor finds a parallel bypass track (MET amplification) that signals the same downstream survival pathways, demonstrating that resistance is selected not created.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = blocked target / drug action / the kept/true thing; CRIMSON #BF3339 = bypass track / resistance / the broken expectation; GOLD #F5D061 = single editor highlight fill only (never text); never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full EGFR biochemistry, NO T790M gatekeeper mutation, NO phenotypic switching, NO pharmacokinetics, NO comparison of different EGFR inhibitor generations beyond naming osimertinib. Manim move: trace (traffic-routing visual). City road map metaphor: main bridge (EGFR) blocked, traffic reroutes through side street (MET) to same destination (RAS/PI3K downstream).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A cancer drug targets one gene precisely. The tumor shrinks dramatically. And eighteen months later it's back -- even though the drug still perfectly blocks its target.

card.copy: Why Blocking the Accelerator Perfectly Can Still Fail
card.sub: when perfect target blockade is not enough

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow in TEAL; title in INK; subtitle in SLATE

beats.narration_text: She is a never-smoker in her mid-fifties. Lung cancer, diagnosed when she had a cough that would not quit. Sequencing finds a mutation in EGFR -- a receptor on the cell surface that tells the cell to grow.

props.headline: She is a never-smoker in her mid-fifties. Lung cancer, diagnosed when…

beats.scene_description: a clinical pathology report on a desk, printed pages, a chest X-ray clipped alongside, the desk lit by a window -- slow push onto the report page, desaturated editorial plate
beats.image_prompt: B02, clinical oncology office, printed pathology report on a desk beside a chest X-ray film, morning window light, desaturated editorial newsprint treatment, flat print reproduction, aged newsprint ground

beats.narration_text: She starts osimertinib -- a drug designed to block that EGFR mutation specifically. Within three months her tumors shrink dramatically. The drug is working exactly as intended.

production_viz.label: tumor before and after osimertinib
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side circles representing tumor mass; left circle large (BEFORE, CRIMSON fill), right circle shrinks to a small dot (AFTER, then TEAL outline); serif label 'osimertinib' above with arrow; '3 months' label below right

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] tumor shrinkage: large crimson circle shrinks to small teal dot

beats.narration_text: Eighteen months later -- imaging shows new growth. A biopsy confirms it. The EGFR mutation is still there, still being blocked. The drug is still doing its job. And yet the tumor is growing.

production_viz.label: the question on screen: EGFR blocked, tumor grows
production_viz.mechanic: EGFR label in TEAL box with a bold X-bar 'BLOCKED'; below it, CRIMSON tumor circle grows again; question card text at bottom: 'EGFR inhibition should stop this tumor. Why is it growing?'

card.copy: EGFR is still present and still blocked. Why is this tumor growing?
card.dek: the gap formula: perfect blockade should stop the tumor -- here it does not

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] EGFR blocked (TEAL X) + tumor re-growing (CRIMSON) + question on screen

beats.narration_text: To understand why this fails, start with what oncogene addiction means. A cancer cell that depends on one mutant receptor has concentrated all of its survival wiring through that one point. Block the point -- the cell has nowhere else to go.

production_viz.label: oncogene addiction: one entry, one path, one destination
production_viz.mechanic: a single vertical trace: EGFR at top (TEAL node) -> single line down -> SURVIVAL node at bottom (TEAL); a TEAL X appears on the line when 'block the point' spoken -- cell icon fades to nothing

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] trace: EGFR -> survival path -> blocked = cell dies

beats.narration_text: This is why EGFR-mutant lung cancer responds so dramatically to osimertinib. The tumor bet everything on one signal. When that signal is cut, the cells have no backup plan -- at first.

production_viz.label: the bet: all signals routed through EGFR
production_viz.mechanic: multiple input lines (growth signals) all converge into EGFR node (TEAL), single exit line to SURVIVAL; a TEAL block closes the EGFR node -- all inputs dead-end; 'no backup plan' serif label appears

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] many inputs -> EGFR -> blocked -> all signals dead-end

beats.narration_text: But that is only the tumor at day one of treatment. A tumor of ten billion cells is not one thing -- it is a population. And in any large population, rare variants exist that were never detected.

production_viz.label: tumor as population: 10 billion cells, rare variants
production_viz.mechanic: a large field of small TEAL squares fills the frame in a count-up grid (representing the tumor); one-in-a-million appear in CRIMSON; label 'one in a million: MET-amplified' appears beside the crimson dots with an arrow

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] isotype grid of tumor population; 1-in-million CRIMSON cells (MET-amplified)

beats.narration_text: Some of those cells have amplified a completely different receptor called MET. MET can activate the same downstream survival signals -- RAS, PI3K -- without going through EGFR at all.

production_viz.label: bypass track: MET routes around blocked EGFR
production_viz.mechanic: the same EGFR -> SURVIVAL trace from B05 on screen; a new CRIMSON line traces from MET node (top right) around the EGFR block and reaches SURVIVAL via a side path labeled 'RAS / PI3K'; two paths visible: TEAL main road blocked, CRIMSON bypass open

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] trace: EGFR blocked (TEAL X); MET bypass draws in CRIMSON reaching SURVIVAL

beats.narration_text: The drug kills ninety-nine point nine nine percent of cells in three months. But the MET-amplified cells do not care. The drug has no effect on MET. Those cells just keep growing.

production_viz.label: selection sweep: TEAL cells die, CRIMSON clone survives
production_viz.mechanic: the large TEAL grid from B07 fades away cell by cell (representing the drug kill); the few CRIMSON MET-amplified cells remain and then expand -- they grow to fill the frame; label 'drug has no effect on MET bypass'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] TEAL cell grid fades; CRIMSON MET-clone expands to fill frame

beats.narration_text: This is the key insight: resistance was not created by the drug. It was selected. The MET-amplified cells existed before treatment started. The drug revealed them by killing everything else.

card.dek: the pre-existing minority survives; the drug is the selection pressure

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] section card: 'Resistance is selected, not created' in INK; CRIMSON accent

beats.narration_text: The tumor at relapse is not a random mutation the drug triggered. It is the winner of a natural selection event that the drug ran. A billion cells competed. One lineage -- the one with a bypass route -- survived.

production_viz.label: clonal selection: before drug, during, after
production_viz.mechanic: horizontal timeline with three columns: BEFORE (large TEAL block + tiny CRIMSON bar), DURING (TEAL block shrinking, CRIMSON bar stable), AFTER (TEAL gone, CRIMSON block dominates); serif labels 'pre-treatment', '3 months', 'relapse'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] timeline: teal population shrinks, crimson clone expands across three stages

beats.narration_text: Here is the math behind this. Her tumor had ten billion cells at treatment start. One in a million carried MET amplification -- so roughly ten thousand MET-amplified cells existed before she took the first pill. That is illustrative, but the scale is real.

production_viz.label: example numbers: 10 billion cells, 1-in-million MET clone
production_viz.mechanic: large number '10,000,000,000' in INK mono font appears; below it '1 in 1,000,000 = MET-amplified'; calculation: 10,000,000,000 / 1,000,000 = 10,000 in CRIMSON mono font; label '~10,000 MET-amplified cells before pill 1' in CRIMSON serif
production_viz.note: all numbers are illustrative -- labeled so in FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] mono numbers: 10B total, 1-in-million rate, ~10,000 pre-existing MET cells

beats.narration_text: Osimertinib kills 99.99 percent of cells over three months -- a near-total response. But 99.99 percent of 10 billion still leaves roughly one million cells alive. And the 10,000 MET-amplified cells? The drug has no effect on them. All 10,000 survive.

production_viz.label: kill math: 99.99% kill rate, MET clone unaffected
production_viz.mechanic: three columns: BEFORE (10B TEAL + 10K CRIMSON), AFTER DRUG (1M TEAL survivors faint + 10K CRIMSON fully colored, labeled 'drug cannot touch MET'); arrow from 10K -> grows to fill CRIMSON block
production_viz.note: numbers are illustrative -- labeled so in FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] kill math: TEAL mass shrinks 99.99%, CRIMSON MET clone remains whole, then expands

beats.narration_text: The question was: how does a tumor grow through perfect target blockade? The answer: perfect blockade of one entry point does not stop a cell population from using another. The bypass track was there before the drug arrived.

card.copy: Perfect blockade of one entry cannot close a bypass that already exists.
card.sub: resistance is selected, not created -- cancer-biology/chapters/05-oncogenes.md

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard: CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow (TEAL); answer one-liner in INK; chapter source in sub

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Biology series.
